text:
Admission Date:  1990-04-30              Discharge Date:   1994-07-02

Date of Birth:  1999-06-12             Sex:   M

Service: SURGERY

Allergies:
Nsaids / Penicillins

Attending:Nicholas
Chief Complaint:
Abdominal pain.

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Vagotomy and antrectomy with retrocolic Billroth II
reconstruction.
Placement of JP drain.


History of Present Illness:
Mr. Wolf is a 75-year-old obese male who presented to the
emergency department with a several-day history of abdominal
pain and fever.  This was followed by the acute onset of
abdominal swelling and tenderness on the day of admission. The
patient did not previously seek medical attention. He was seen
in the emergency room where a CT scan of the abdomen was
performed which suggested leakage of dye from the duodenum.  The
patient was felt to
have an ulcer of some sort in the gastroduodenal area, probably
gastric because of his lack of symptoms in the past which had
perforated into the lesser sac and subsequently the lesser sac
collection had perforated free. The patient was
taken to the operating theater emergently after approximately 6
liters of lactated Ringer's and receiving antibiotics and
placement of a nasogastric tube.


Past Medical History:
Hypertension, BPH, atrial fibrillation, idopathic pericardial
effusion, s/pt appendectomy, s/p pericardiocentesis.

Family History:
Noncontributory.

Physical Exam:
(On Admission)
VS: 101.6, 86, 108/44, 20, 95%2L NC
NAD, A&Ox3, relaxed
PERRL, EOMI
RRR, CTAB
ABD soft, obese. Sl TTP bilateral lower quadrants, +BS, no R/G.
No c/c/e
No focal deficits.

CT ABDOMEN W/O CONTRAST; CT PELVIS W/O CONTRAST   Clip #
[**Clip Number (Radiology) 30686**]
IMPRESSION:
1.  Perforated duodenal ulcer.  Large amount of free air and
free peritoneal fluid, with leaking oral contrast which pools in
the lesser sac, in the pouch of 4578 Howard Courts
West Meganstad, NV 74184-9141, and about the liver.
Emergent surgical consultation is reccomended.
2.  Dense area of consolidation in right lower lobe.  This is
incompletely
assessed.  Attention to this area should be paid on future chest
radiographs. If this does not resolve, a dedicated CT scanning
of the chest is recommended.
3.  Diverticulosis without evidence of acute diverticulitis.
4.  Hiatal hernia.
------------

CHEST (PORTABLE AP)   Clip # [**Clip Number (Radiology) 30687**]
FINDINGS:  Compared with 11/20 at 11:16 p.m., ETT has been
removed.  There has been considerable partial interval clearing
of the bilateral lower lobe atelectatic changes.  The upper lung
fields are clear.  No overt CHF.
---------------

CTA CHEST W&W/O C &RECONS   Clip # [**Clip Number (Radiology) 30688**]
IMPRESSION:
1.  No pulmonary emboli visualized.
2.  Multiple subcentimeter lymph nodes within the mediastinum,
unchanged from prior examination of 1970-06-09, most likely
hyperplastic/benign in etiology.
3.  Dense opacification of the anterolateral and posterior
segments of the
right lower lobe as well as of the posterior segment of the left
lower lobe, compatible with pneumonia.







Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. Wolf was admitted to the hospital following emergent
laparotomy and repair of a perforated duodenum on the day of
admission. He was initially admitted to the SICU after requiring
pressors in the OR. On hospital day two his sedation was
discontinued and he continued to improve. He was extubated on
post-operative day 2 but had continued issues with fluid
balance, requiring a lasix drip and frequent repletion of
electrolytes. His fluid issues slowly resolved after
discontinuation of lasix and he was transferred to the floor on
hospital day 5, post-operative day 4. Mr. Wolf had an episode
of abrupt tachycardia and decreased O2sats on POD#5. He had a
CTA that was negative for PE, but showed some RLL consolidation.
His sats improved to 99% (from 87%) after spontaneously coughing
up a mucous plug. He has tolerated a return to a regular diet
without difficulty, and his JP drain was pulled on HD #7 after a
drain amylase showed no evidence of leak. He was evaluated by
physical therapy and felt safe for discharge to home.

Medications on Admission:
atenolol, diovan, flomax


Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
Duodenal perforation, status-post exploratory laparotomy and
repair.
Pneumonia.


Discharge Condition:
Good.


Discharge Instructions:
You should call your doctor or return to the emergency
department if you experience any of the following: fever,
worsening abdominal pain, nausea or vomiting such that you are
unable to tolerate food or drink, shortness of Cooper, Melanie new
redness, pain, or discharge from your wounds, or any
new/concerning symptom.

You should not do any heavy lifting for approximatly six weeks.
You may shower, though it is not recommended to soak your wounds
(i.e., in a bathtub) until you are seen in follow up.

Please take all of your prescribed medications for the duration
indicated. You are being discharged with prescriptions for three
antibiotics, take the entire course of these antibiotics. Do not
stop early, even if you begin to feel better. Do not stop any
medications unless instructed to do so by your doctor.
